nativeRange.habitat: Coastal marine, estuarine, and tidal river mouths along the Pacific coast; adults are principally marine, juveniles use brackish and occasionally freshwater estuaries as nursery habitat

decorPreferences: open swimming space — avoid excessive rockwork
robust hardscape (driftwood, large smooth rocks) — plants not suitable as adults require brackish/marine
strong filtration — large messy predator with high bioload

diet.notes: Piscivorous and invertebrate-feeding carnivore. Accepts sinking carnivore pellets, large frozen foods (krill, shrimp, lance fish, whitebait, mussels), and earthworms. Juveniles will take frozen bloodworm and brine shrimp; transition adults to larger chunky foods. Feeds predominantly at night in the wild; target-feed if housed with faster tankmates. Avoid feeder goldfish — nutritionally poor and disease risk.

goodWith: large euryhaline fish of similar size (monos, scats, archerfish, large target perch)
figure-eight puffers (as adults in brackish — with caution)
avoidWith: small fish that will be eaten (anything under ~8–10 cm)
small invertebrates (shrimp, snails — will be consumed)
freshwater-only species unable to tolerate brackish progression

Wikimedia Commons image showing juvenile Colombian Shark Catfish, the life stage typically seen in trade.

summary: The Colombian Shark Catfish is a large euryhaline ariid catfish sold as a freshwater juvenile but requiring a full transition to brackish then marine conditions as it matures. Grows to 35 cm, has venomous dorsal and pectoral spines, and is a remarkable paternal mouthbrooder incubating golf-ball-sized eggs orally. Specialist-only husbandry commitment required.
careNotes: The Colombian Shark Catfish (Ariopsis seemanni) is one of the most consistently mis-sold fish in the freshwater trade. Juveniles appear in shops at 5–8 cm labelled as freshwater catfish, capitalising on their shark-like silhouette and silvery sheen. In reality, this species is a coastal marine ariid catfish; wild adults live in the sea, and juveniles use estuaries only as temporary nursery habitat. Long-term survival in pure freshwater is not possible — fish kept in fresh water deteriorate over months, developing osmotic stress, immune compromise, and shortened lifespans. The correct husbandry trajectory is: juvenile freshwater acclimation (SG 1.000–1.002, tenable short-term only) → subadult brackish (SG 1.005–1.010) → adult marine or near-marine (SG 1.015–1.025). Transitions must be gradual (raise SG by no more than 0.001–0.002 per day). A species-appropriate setup must be planned from the outset; the keeper cannot simply 'upgrade later' — the salt requirement increases with growth and the fish cannot be rehomed easily at 30 cm+. Tank size must be substantial: a minimum 400 L (180 cm footprint) is recommended for a small group of three, with powerful filtration as these are large, messy, high-bioload carnivores. They are active mid-water and surface swimmers and must have a tightly secured lid — escape risk is high. The venomous dorsal and pectoral spines are capable of delivering a painful sting; handle with extreme caution using thick gloves or a container, never bare hands. Colombian Shark Catfish are shoaling fish and should be kept in groups of three or more; solitary individuals are prone to stress. Tankmates must be large, robust, and euryhaline — suitable partners include monodactylids (monos), scatophagids (scats), archerfish, and other large brackish specialists. Any freshwater-only species cannot share their tank long-term, as water parameters must trend toward marine. These fish are copper-sensitive like most catfishes — never use copper-based medications. Water quality must be pristine; high nitrates accelerate the health decline seen in inappropriately fresh water.
breedingNotes: Ariopsis seemanni is a paternal mouthbrooder with one of the most remarkable reproductive strategies in the family Ariidae. Males incubate a small clutch of very large, yolk-rich eggs — often described as golf-ball sized — entirely within their oral cavity for approximately two months. During brooding the male cannot feed. Fertilised eggs and even early-stage fry are brooded in the mouth until the young are advanced and self-sufficient. Captive breeding has not been reliably reported in home aquaria; the species almost certainly requires near-marine or marine salinity and very large tanks to spawn naturally. The species' mouthbrooding strategy produces a small number of large, well-developed offspring rather than the mass spawning typical of open-water broadcast spawners.

primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 35 cm), water parameters, native range (Pacific coast Mexico to Peru), habitat (marine/estuarine), euryhaline biology, salinity progression requirements, mouthbrooding behaviour, venomous spines, diet, compatibility, and husbandry guidance. Authoritative species-level treatment.

additional.name: FishBase — Ariopsis seemanni
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 35 cm TL), taxonomy (Ariidae, Siluriformes), native range (Pacific Mexico to Peru), habitat (coastal marine), temperature range, and life history data. Confirmed mouthbrooder status (paternal oral incubation of large eggs).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Colombian Shark Catfish
additional.notes: Trade-facing care guide. Confirmed juvenile freshwater tolerance, brackish progression requirement (SG 1.005–1.010 for adults), specialist-only suitability, large adult size, venomous spines, and carnivore diet. Noted common mis-selling as freshwater community fish.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Ariopsis seemanni
additional.notes: Supplementary care and trade notes. Confirmed 'Colombian Shark' trade name, brackish/marine adult requirements, minimum group of three, high bioload, escape risk, and the problem of mis-selling in freshwater community tanks.

additional.name: FishBase — Ariopsis seemanni (mouthbrooding detail)
additional.notes: Reproduction summary confirming paternal mouthbrooding (oral incubation), small clutch of large eggs, extended brooding period during which male does not feed.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; males orally incubate a small clutch of very large (golf-ball sized) eggs for ~2 months without feeding. Breeding has not been reliably achieved in home aquaria. Likely requires near-marine or marine salinity, very large tanks, and undisturbed male brooding periods.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Frequently mis-sold as freshwater community fish; actually a BRACKISH→MARINE ariid catfish growing to 35cm+. Juvenile freshwater tolerance is short-term; long-term keeping requires brackish (SG 1.005-1.010) to marine setup. Conscientious aquarist commitment to lifelong euryhaline husbandry.
